---
title: "4.1.2 Operating systems"
subtitle: "Overview and interfaces · P155-P157"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .figure-placeholder { border: 1px dashed #8aa0bf; border-radius: 8px; background: #f4f8ff; color: #40526b; padding: 1.1em 1.3em; margin: 0.8em 0; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.74em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.34em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 4 Directory / 返回 Unit 4 目录](../../index.html)

## Operating Systems

### Why an operating system is needed

To enable computer systems to function correctly and allow users to communicate with computer systems, an <span class="term">operating system</span> must be installed.

An operating system provides:

::: {.tight-list}
- the <span class="mark">environment</span> in which applications can be run
- a useable interface between humans and computer
- a way to disguise the complexity of computer software and hardware
:::

## Operating Systems

### Examples and storage

Common examples include Microsoft Windows, Apple Mac OS, Google Android and Apple IOS.

Most computers store the operating system on an HDD or SSD because operating systems tend to be very large programs.

Mobile phones and tablets store the operating system on a solid state device because they are too small to accommodate an HDD.

## Operating System Functions

### Figure 4.7 overview

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 4.7 Operating system functions screenshot placeholder
:::

Figure 4.7 lists functions such as HCI, security, multitasking, memory management, hardware peripheral management, file management and user account management.

## Human Computer Interface

### CLI and GUI

The <span class="term">human computer interface (HCI)</span> is in the form of a:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">Command Line Interface (CLI)</span>
- <span class="term">Graphical User Interface (GUI)</span>
:::

A CLI requires typed instructions. A GUI uses pictures or symbols called icons.

## Command Line Interface

### Direct communication

A <span class="term">CLI</span> requires commands to be typed in to choose options, open software, save or load a file.

The user must learn commands, and typing them each time is slow.

The advantage is that the user is in <span class="mark">direct communication</span> with the computer and is not restricted to pre-determined options.

## Graphical User Interface

### Icons and applications

A <span class="term">GUI</span> allows the user to interact with a computer, MP3 player, gaming device or mobile phone using icons.

Selecting an icon can automatically load the application ready to be used.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 4.8 GUI icons on a mobile phone screenshot placeholder
:::

## WIMP Interfaces

### Windows, icons, menu and pointing device

One common GUI technology is <span class="term">WIMP</span> (windows icons menu and pointing device).

::: {.tight-list}
- a mouse controls a cursor
- icons are selected to open or run windows
- each window contains an application
- several windows can be open at the same time
:::

## WIMP Interfaces

### Windows manager and Figure 4.9

A <span class="term">windows manager</span> looks after interaction between windows, applications, pointing devices and the cursor's position.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 4.9 Windows screen showing icons screenshot placeholder
:::

## Post-WIMP Interactions

### Touchscreen actions

Devices such as mobile phones and tablets increasingly use touch screens and <span class="term">post-WIMP interactions</span>.

Fingers are in contact with the screen, allowing actions such as:

::: {.tight-list}
- pinching
- rotating
:::

## Table 4.1

### CLI comparison

::: {.clean-table}
| CLI advantages | CLI disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Direct communication with the computer | User needs to learn commands |
| Not restricted to pre-determined options | Commands take time and can be error-prone |
| Can alter computer configuration settings | Commands need correct format and spelling |
| Uses a small amount of computer memory |  |
:::

## Table 4.1

### GUI comparison

::: {.clean-table}
| GUI advantages | GUI disadvantages |
|---|---|
| User does not need to learn commands | Uses more memory than CLI |
| More user-friendly; icons represent applications | User is limited to screen icons |
| Pointing device or touch screen can launch applications | Needs an operating system to operate |
:::

## Interface Users

### Who would use each interface?

<span class="term">CLI</span> is used by a programmer, analyst or technician needing direct communication with a computer.

<span class="term">GUI</span> is used by an end-user who runs software, plays games or stores and manipulates photographs.

## Classroom Check

### Explain OS purpose and interfaces

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- an operating system provides an application environment and a human-computer interface
- operating systems disguise software and hardware complexity
- CLI uses typed commands and gives direct communication
- GUI uses icons and is more user-friendly
- WIMP uses windows, icons, menu and pointing device
- post-WIMP uses touch actions such as pinching and rotating
:::

## Bridge

### Next: OS management functions

The next deck covers memory, security, hardware peripheral and file management.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 4 Directory / 返回 Unit 4 目录](../../index.html)
